description: Bold Nigerian tomato stew made with fresh or frozen Titus mackerel. Faster than meat stew but demands careful handling — fish breaks easily and the base must be properly fried before the fish ever touches it.

ingredients.name: Titus mackerel (fresh or frozen)
ingredients.note: Fresh is best. Frozen works — defrost fully and pat dry before seasoning. Cut into steaks about 4cm thick.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper (ata rodo)
ingredients.note: Fish stew does not need excessive heat — 1 is enough for most.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 1 for seasoning the fish, 1 into the blend, 1 sliced for frying the base.

ingredients.name: vegetable oil
ingredients.note: Less oil than meat stew — fish releases its own fat into the base.

ingredients.name: tomato paste
ingredients.note: For depth of colour and flavour.

ingredients.name: curry powder
ingredients.note: For both seasoning the fish and flavouring the stew.

ingredients.name: bay leaves
ingredients.note: Add to the stew base — subtle herby depth.

ingredients.name: seasoning cubes
ingredients.note: 1 for the fish, 1 for the stew base.

ingredients.name: ground crayfish
ingredients.note: Particularly complementary to fish stew — deepens the seafood dimension.

ingredients.name: garlic
ingredients.note: For seasoning the fish. Do not add to the stew base — it overpowers the fish.

ingredients.name: lemon or lime juice
ingredients.note: For washing and marinating the fish. Removes the fishy smell significantly.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Season the fish well. Season the stew base separately and carefully — fish adds its own salt.

steps.title: Clean and Season the Fish
steps.instruction: Fish stew lives or dies by how well the fish is prepared before it ever sees heat — the cleaning, drying, and seasoning stages are where the work happens. If using whole fish, gut and clean them thoroughly, removing the head if preferred. Cut into steaks about 4cm thick — thinner than this and they will fall apart the moment they hit the hot oil; thicker and the centre may not cook through properly. Wash all the steaks in cold water mixed with lemon or lime juice, turning each piece and rubbing gently — this acid wash significantly reduces the strong fishy smell and firms the flesh slightly. Rinse with clean water and then pat every surface of every piece completely dry with paper towels — this step is not optional. Season with curry powder, white pepper, minced garlic if using, 1 crushed seasoning cube, and salt. Let the fish rest for at least 15 minutes.
micro_steps: If using whole fish, gut and clean thoroughly under cold running water — remove the gills and any dark membrane inside the cavity
Cut each fish into steaks approximately 4cm thick — use a sharp, heavy knife and cut in one clean motion
Fill a large bowl with cold water and add 2 tablespoons of lemon or lime juice
Place all the fish steaks into the acidulated water and turn each piece gently — leave for 2 minutes
Remove the steaks and rinse each one under cold running water
Pat every surface of every steak completely dry with paper towels — press firmly to remove all moisture
Place the dried steaks on a plate or tray
Add ½ tsp curry powder, ½ tsp white pepper, the minced garlic, 1 crushed seasoning cube, and a generous pinch of salt
Rub the seasoning gently into every surface of each steak — do not press hard or the flesh will break
Leave to rest at room temperature for 15 minutes before frying
prep_notes: Blend the tomatoes, tatashe, scotch bonnet, and 1 onion before starting — the base needs to go on the moment the fish is fried
If using frozen fish, defrost it fully in the fridge the night before — never defrost in warm water, it softens the flesh and makes it fall apart
Have a wide spatula or fish slice ready before frying — a spoon will break the steaks
Dry the fish as thoroughly as possible — any moisture on the surface will cause violent oil splatter and prevent a proper crust from forming
steps.sensory_cue: Properly cleaned and seasoned fish steaks should smell clean, fresh, and aromatic — the lemon wash removes the raw fishiness and leaves a mild, neutral scent with the seasoning sitting on top. The flesh should feel firm and hold its shape when handled, not soft or mushy. After resting, the seasoning should look like it has been absorbed into the surface rather than sitting on top.
steps.failure_risk: Wet fish is the single biggest cause of fish stew problems — it causes dangerous oil splatter when frying, prevents any crust from forming, and causes the fish to steam and fall apart instead of searing. Pat the fish as dry as you possibly can, and if you have time, leave the seasoned pieces uncovered in the fridge for 30 minutes — the cold air dries the surface further.

steps.title: Shallow-Fry the Fish
steps.instruction: Heat half the vegetable oil in your widest, heaviest pan over high heat until it is very hot — shimmering and just on the edge of smoking. Carefully lower the fish steaks into the oil one at a time, tilting them away from you as you place them to direct any splatter away. They should sizzle loudly and aggressively the moment they make contact with the oil. Fry completely undisturbed for 3–4 minutes until a golden-brown crust has firmly set on the underside. Do not nudge, move, or press the fish during this time. Flip carefully using a wide spatula, sliding it fully under each steak before lifting. Fry the second side for 3 minutes, then remove to a plate. The fried crust is the structural layer that keeps the fish intact through the rest of the cooking — it must be well-formed.
micro_steps: Pour half the vegetable oil — about 40ml — into your widest pan
Heat over high heat until the oil shimmers and a tiny drop of water sizzles and spits immediately
Lower the first fish steak into the oil carefully, tilting it away from you
Add the remaining steaks one at a time in a single uncrowded layer — do not overlap
The fish should sizzle loudly the moment it hits the oil — if it does not, the oil is not hot enough
Leave completely undisturbed for 3–4 minutes — do not touch, press, or move
After 3 minutes, slide a wide spatula under one steak to check — it should lift cleanly with a golden-brown underside
If it sticks or the underside is pale, leave it for another 30–60 seconds before checking again
Flip each steak carefully with a wide spatula — one smooth, confident motion
Fry the second side for 3 minutes
Remove each steak carefully to a plate — do not stack them
Leave the fish-flavoured oil in the pan for the next step
steps.sensory_cue: The fish should sizzle aggressively and loudly from the moment it hits the oil — a loud, active sizzle means the temperature is right. The finished crust should be golden-brown, slightly firm, and hold the steak together as a single unit. The kitchen should smell beautifully savoury and toasty, not raw or steamy. If it smells steamy rather than frying, the oil was not hot enough.
steps.failure_risk: Moving or pressing the fish before the crust has fully set is the fastest way to turn fish steaks into fish pieces. The crust needs uninterrupted contact with the hot oil to firm up and set — it acts as structural support for the rest of the cook. If a steak sticks when you try to flip it, it is telling you it is not ready — wait 30 more seconds and try again. Do not cover the pan at any point — trapped steam softens and destroys the crust.

steps.title: Build the Stew Base
steps.instruction: Without cleaning the pan, add the remaining oil to the fish drippings already there — the fish fat left behind is flavour you want to keep. Fry the sliced onion over medium-high heat directly in the fish-flavoured oil, scraping up any bits from the bottom, until soft and deep golden. Add tomato paste and fry vigorously for 3 minutes, stirring constantly, until it darkens to a deep brick colour. Pour in the blended tomatoes, tatashe, and scotch bonnet, increase to high heat, and fry the base — stirring every 90 seconds — until it is thick, concentrated, and the oil has clearly risen to the surface. This full frying of the base is critical: once the fish goes in, you cannot stir aggressively again.
micro_steps: Add the remaining vegetable oil to the fish drippings in the pan — do not clean or wipe the pan
Heat over medium-high and add the sliced onion rings
Stir immediately, scraping up any fish residue from the pan bottom into the onions
Fry for 4–5 minutes until the onions are soft and deeply golden
Add 2 tablespoons of tomato paste and stir vigorously
Fry the paste for 3 minutes, stirring constantly, until it deepens to a dark brick-red colour
Pour in all the blended tomato, tatashe, and scotch bonnet mixture
Increase heat to high and stir everything together
Fry the base at high heat, stirring every 90 seconds, for 15–20 minutes
The base is fully ready when it is thick and paste-like, dark brick-red, and oil has risen clearly to the surface
The raw tomato smell should be completely gone — replaced by a deep, fried, slightly smoky aroma with a subtle seafood note from the fish oil
steps.sensory_cue: The finished base should be thick, paste-like, and dark orange-red, with the oil clearly floating on the surface. The fish fat left in the pan from frying adds a subtle seafood note to the base that is a distinctive quality of well-made fish stew. Any raw or sour tomato smell must be completely gone before proceeding.
steps.failure_risk: Fish stew base must be completely and thoroughly fried before the fish is added — once the fish goes in, you lose the ability to stir aggressively without breaking it. This is your only window to cook the base properly. A under-fried base produces a sour, watery stew that the delicate fish cannot save.

steps.title: Season the Base
steps.instruction: Reduce the heat to medium. Add the remaining curry powder, thyme, ground crayfish, bay leaves, and the second crushed seasoning cube, stirring each one in thoroughly. Now taste the base before adding any salt — Titus mackerel is naturally quite salty and will release more of its natural salts into the stew as it finishes cooking, so the base needs far less added salt than a meat stew. Add a tiny pinch only, if needed at all. Add about ¼ cup of water and stir in to loosen the base slightly — fish stew should be saucier and more fluid than meat stew, giving the fish something to gently poach in rather than sitting on a thick paste.
micro_steps: Reduce heat to medium
Add 1 tsp curry powder and stir in
Add 1 tsp dried thyme and stir in
Add 1 tablespoon of ground crayfish if using and stir — the aroma will deepen noticeably
Add the bay leaves and stir in
Crush the remaining seasoning cube to powder and add — stir until dissolved
Taste the base carefully before considering salt — it may already be sufficiently seasoned
Add salt only in the tiniest pinch if it genuinely needs it
Add approximately ¼ cup of water and stir in to loosen the base slightly
The base should now look saucy enough to coat the fish without being a thick paste
Bring to a gentle simmer over medium heat before adding the fish
steps.sensory_cue: The seasoned base should smell full, complex, and deeply savoury — the crayfish deepens the seafood dimension noticeably. The consistency should be looser than meat stew — saucy enough that when you tilt the pan, the base flows freely. It should taste well-seasoned but not salty, because the fish is about to contribute more.
steps.failure_risk: Over-salting at this stage is very easy and very hard to fix once the fish is in. Titus mackerel carries significant natural salt and will release it into the base as it cooks — a base that tastes perfectly salted before the fish goes in will almost certainly be over-salted by the time it is done. Season conservatively and plan to adjust only at the very end.

steps.title: Add Fish and Finish Gently
steps.instruction: This final step requires the gentlest possible handling — the fried crust holds the fish together but it is not invincible. Carefully lay each fried fish steak into the stew base, spacing them out so they are not stacked. Do not stir. Instead, use a large spoon to ladle the sauce up and over the top of each fish piece to coat them from above. Cover the pot with a lid and cook on low heat for 10–12 minutes. Halfway through, use a wide spatula to turn each piece once — slide it fully under before lifting, never use a spoon. Remove the bay leaves, do a final careful salt taste, and serve immediately. The stew around the fish will have deepened in colour and the fish will have released its natural juices into the base.
micro_steps: Reduce heat to low before adding the fish
Carefully lay each fried fish steak into the stew base — do not stack or crowd
Do not stir — use a large spoon to ladle sauce up over the top of each piece
Cover the pot with a lid
Cook on low heat for 5 minutes
At the 5-minute mark, uncover and use a wide spatula to gently turn each piece once
Slide the spatula fully under each steak before lifting — a spoon will break it
Ladle sauce over the newly exposed top side of each piece
Cover again and cook for a further 5–7 minutes
Press a piece gently with your finger — it should flake slightly and feel cooked through
Remove and discard both bay leaves
Taste the stew and adjust salt very carefully if needed — add in the smallest possible increments
Serve immediately — fish stew does not hold well and degrades quickly off the heat
steps.sensory_cue: The stew surrounding the fish should look rich, glossy, and deeply coloured — noticeably richer than before the fish went in, as the fish juices have blended into the base. The fish steaks should be fully cooked through — press one gently and it should just begin to flake. The aroma should be bold, deeply savoury, and layered — the smell of the sea meeting a properly fried tomato base.
steps.failure_risk: Any stirring in this step will break the fish steaks apart — use only a spatula for turning and a spoon for basting, never for mixing. Do not overcook beyond 12 minutes total — fish goes from perfectly cooked to dry and crumbling very quickly, and there is no way back once it is overdone. Serve as soon as it is ready.
